---
title: "Chapter 16 — 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 15 · ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks](ch15.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [17 · 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models →](ch17.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are the family of neural networks built to exploit the structure of grid-like data — most famously images, where nearby pixels are related and the same visual pattern can appear anywhere in the frame. By replacing the dense "everything connects to everything" wiring of plain networks with small, repeated, location-aware filters, CNNs learn visual features efficiently and powered the deep learning revolution in computer vision from 2012 onward. They sit squarely in the deep learning part of the field, as the standard architecture for spatial data before Vision Transformers arrived as an alternative.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Deep learning architecture for grid-structured data · used for image classification, detection, segmentation, and any signal with local spatial or temporal structure · the one key idea is *the convolution*: a small filter shared across all positions, so patterns are learned once and detected everywhere.
:::

## 16.1 — Why convolution for images

Imagine handing a photo to an ordinary fully-connected network. A modest 224×224 colour image has $224 \times 224 \times 3 \approx 150{,}000$ input values. The very first dense layer with, say, 1,000 neurons would need 150 million weights — and it would have to learn what a "vertical edge" looks like *separately* for every single pixel location, because each weight is tied to one specific input position. That is wasteful, and it throws away an obvious fact: a cat in the top-left corner is still a cat in the bottom-right.

Convolution fixes this with three structural priors baked into the architecture. A **prior** here just means an assumption about the world wired into the network's shape, so it does not have to learn that assumption from data.

- **Local connectivity (local patterns).** Each neuron looks only at a small patch of the image (say 3×3 pixels), not the whole thing. Meaningful visual primitives — edges, corners, textures — are local, so a neuron needs only a local window to detect them.
- **Parameter sharing.** The *same* small set of weights (a **filter**) is slid across every position in the image. One filter that detects a horizontal edge is reused everywhere, so the network learns it once instead of 50,000 times. This is what slashes the parameter count.
- **Translation equivariance.** Because the filter is applied identically at every location, shifting the input shifts the output the same way. Combined with pooling, this gives approximate **translation invariance**: the network's verdict ("cat present") barely changes when the cat moves.

The distinction between those last two words is worth pinning down, because they are easy to confuse. **Equivariance** means "move the input, the output moves with it" — the feature map of a shifted cat is just the shifted feature map. **Invariance** means "move the input, the output does not change" — the final label stays "cat." Convolution gives you equivariance for free; pooling and global aggregation are what convert that equivariance into the invariance a classifier actually wants.

::: {.callout-tip}
The mental model: a dense layer asks "what is the value at pixel 12,043?"; a convolutional layer asks "is *this small pattern* present, and where?" The second question is the one that generalizes.
:::

## 16.2 — Filters, kernels, stride, padding, and the output-size formula

A **kernel** (or **filter**) is a small grid of learnable weights — commonly 3×3 or 5×5. Convolution slides this kernel over the input; at each position it computes a dot product between the kernel weights and the patch of input underneath, producing a single output number. Sweep over all positions and you get a 2-D grid of responses called a **feature map**: bright where the pattern matched, dark where it did not.

Concretely, for a single output pixel the operation is

$$ y_{i,j} = \sum_{m}\sum_{n} x_{\,i+m,\;j+n}\; \cdot\; w_{m,n} \; + \; b $$

where $w$ is the kernel, $b$ a bias, and the sums run over the kernel's small window. (Strictly this is *cross-correlation*; deep learning calls it convolution and the learned weights absorb the difference.)

Two knobs control how the kernel sweeps:

- **Stride** $s$: how many pixels the kernel jumps each step. Stride 1 visits every position; stride 2 skips every other one, halving the output's width and height and acting as a built-in downsample.
- **Padding** $p$: how many rings of (usually zero) pixels are added around the border. Without padding, output shrinks every layer and edge pixels get under-sampled; *"same" padding* adds just enough zeros to keep the output the same size as the input.

The output spatial size follows one formula worth memorizing. For an input of size $W$, kernel $K$, padding $P$, stride $S$:

$$ W_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{W - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1 $$

For example, a 224-wide input with a 3×3 kernel ($K=3$), same padding ($P=1$), stride 1 gives $(224 - 3 + 2)/1 + 1 = 224$ — size preserved. Switch to stride 2 and you get $\lfloor 223/2 \rfloor + 1 = 112$ — halved.

Here is the mechanism from scratch, no framework:

```python
import numpy as np

def conv2d(x, w, b=0, stride=1, pad=0):
    x = np.pad(x, pad)                       # zero-pad border
    K = w.shape[0]                           # assume square kernel
    H = (x.shape[0] - K) // stride + 1       # output-size formula
    Wd = (x.shape[1] - K) // stride + 1
    out = np.zeros((H, Wd))
    for i in range(H):                       # slide over rows
        for j in range(Wd):                  # slide over cols
            patch = x[i*stride:i*stride+K, j*stride:j*stride+K]
            out[i, j] = np.sum(patch * w) + b   # dot product = one pixel
    return out

# sanity check: a horizontal-edge kernel fires on a top/bottom split
img = np.vstack([np.zeros((3,4)), np.ones((3,4))])
edge = np.array([[ 1, 1, 1],[0,0,0],[-1,-1,-1]])
assert conv2d(img, edge).max() > 0   # responds at the boundary
```

::: {.callout-warning}
A common confusion: stride and pooling both downsample, but a *bigger kernel* does **not** shrink the output much on its own with "same" padding — it widens the **receptive field** (Section 16.4) instead. Mixing these up leads to architectures whose tensor shapes don't line up.
:::

## 16.3 — Feature maps and channels

Real inputs are not flat grids; a colour image has three **channels** (red, green, blue) stacked in depth. So a kernel is not really 2-D — it spans the full input depth. A 3×3 kernel on an RGB image is actually a 3×3×3 block of weights, and its dot product sums across all three channels to produce one output number. One kernel, however deep, still yields a single 2-D feature map.

To detect many patterns, a convolutional layer holds *many* kernels — say 64 of them. Each produces its own feature map, and they are stacked to form an output with 64 channels. So the layer's job is a mapping from $C_{\text{in}}$ input channels to $C_{\text{out}}$ output channels, and its weight tensor has shape $(C_{\text{out}}, C_{\text{in}}, K, K)$ with $C_{\text{out}} \cdot C_{\text{in}} \cdot K^2$ parameters — independent of image size, which is the whole point.

The intuition for depth: early layers have a few channels detecting simple things (edges at various angles). Deeper layers have many more channels, each combining lower-level maps into richer concepts — edges become textures, textures become object parts, parts become objects. The spatial grid shrinks as you go deep (via stride/pooling) while the channel count grows, trading "where" for "what."

A special case, the **1×1 convolution**, has no spatial extent at all: it mixes channels at each pixel independently. It is the cheap workhorse for changing channel count — squeezing 256 channels down to 64 before an expensive operation, then back up — and shows up everywhere in efficient architectures.

## 16.4 — Pooling and the receptive field

**Pooling** downsamples a feature map by summarizing each small neighbourhood with a single value. **Max pooling** over 2×2 windows keeps only the largest activation in each window, halving width and height; **average pooling** takes the mean. There are no learned weights — it is a fixed reduction.

Pooling does two jobs. It shrinks the spatial grid, cutting computation and memory in deeper layers. And it grants a little **translation invariance**: if the strongest edge response shifts by one pixel, max pooling over its neighbourhood often returns the same value, so small wobbles in input don't ripple through the network.

A close cousin worth knowing is **global average pooling**: instead of a 2×2 window, it averages each feature map down to a single number, collapsing a $C \times H \times W$ tensor to $C$ values. Modern networks use it in place of the old flatten-then-huge-dense-layer ending — it has no parameters, tolerates any input size, and is far less prone to overfitting. It is the standard bridge from the convolutional stack to the final classifier.

This connects to the **receptive field** — the region of the original input that a given deep neuron can "see." A neuron in the first 3×3 conv layer sees a 3×3 patch. Stack a second 3×3 layer and each neuron there sees a 3×3 window of first-layer outputs, which traces back to a 5×5 patch of the input. Add pooling or stride and the receptive field grows fast. By the deep layers, a single neuron's receptive field can cover most of the image — which is exactly why deep stacks can recognize whole objects despite each individual filter being tiny.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A["Input<br/>224x224x3"] --> B["Conv 3x3<br/>64 maps"]
    B --> C["Pool 2x2<br/>112x112"]
    C --> D["Conv 3x3<br/>128 maps"]
    D --> E["Pool 2x2<br/>56x56"]
    E --> F["... deeper ..."]
    F --> G["Global<br/>avg pool"]
    G --> H["Dense<br/>softmax"]
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Rule of thumb for designing a stack: two stacked 3×3 convs cover the same 5×5 receptive field as one 5×5 conv, but with fewer parameters ($2 \cdot 9 = 18$ vs $25$ per channel pair) and an extra nonlinearity in between. This is exactly why VGG chose to stack small kernels.
:::

A kernel sliding over the input, producing one feature-map pixel per position:

<svg viewBox="0 0 360 170" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="9">
  <text x="10" y="14" font-size="11">input grid</text>
  <g stroke="currentColor" fill="none" opacity="0.5">
    <rect x="10" y="20" width="120" height="120"/>
    <line x1="40" y1="20" x2="40" y2="140"/><line x1="70" y1="20" x2="70" y2="140"/><line x1="100" y1="20" x2="100" y2="140"/>
    <line x1="10" y1="50" x2="130" y2="50"/><line x1="10" y1="80" x2="130" y2="80"/><line x1="10" y1="110" x2="130" y2="110"/>
  </g>
  <rect x="10" y="20" width="90" height="90" fill="currentColor" opacity="0.18"/>
  <rect x="10" y="20" width="90" height="90" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="30" y="68" font-size="8">3x3 kernel</text>
  <path d="M132 65 C170 65 175 65 200 65" stroke="currentColor" fill="none" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
  <defs><marker id="a" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 z" fill="currentColor"/></marker></defs>
  <text x="210" y="14" font-size="11">feature map</text>
  <g stroke="currentColor" fill="none" opacity="0.5">
    <rect x="210" y="20" width="120" height="120"/>
    <line x1="240" y1="20" x2="240" y2="140"/><line x1="270" y1="20" x2="270" y2="140"/><line x1="300" y1="20" x2="300" y2="140"/>
    <line x1="210" y1="50" x2="330" y2="50"/><line x1="210" y1="80" x2="330" y2="80"/><line x1="210" y1="110" x2="330" y2="110"/>
  </g>
  <rect x="210" y="20" width="30" height="30" fill="currentColor" opacity="0.6"/>
  <text x="120" y="160" font-size="9">each kernel position → one output activation</text>
</svg>

## 16.5 — Classic architectures, in order

The history of CNNs is a history of going deeper while fighting the problems that depth creates. Reading the milestones in order is the fastest way to understand *why* modern networks look the way they do.

- **LeNet-5 (1998).** LeCun's network for reading handwritten digits — two conv+pool stages then dense layers. Tiny (~60K parameters) but it established the conv → pool → conv → pool → dense template that everything else builds on.
- **AlexNet (2012).** The network that won ImageNet and lit the fuse. Same template, far bigger, trained on two GPUs. Its key practical moves: **ReLU** activations (which train much faster than sigmoids), **dropout** for regularization, and heavy data augmentation. Proof that deep CNNs plus data plus GPUs beat hand-engineered vision features decisively.
- **VGG (2014).** Radically simple: stack only 3×3 convs and 2×2 pools, 16–19 layers deep. Showed depth-via-small-kernels works and gave the field a clean, reusable backbone — at the cost of being heavy (~138M parameters, most of them in the dense layers).
- **Inception / GoogLeNet (2014).** Asked "which kernel size?" and answered "all of them." An **Inception module** runs 1×1, 3×3, and 5×5 convs plus pooling in parallel and concatenates the results, with 1×1 convs first to cut cost. More accurate than VGG with ~12× fewer parameters.

The wall everyone hit next was that simply stacking more layers made networks *worse*, not better — training error went up, a sign of an optimization failure rather than overfitting. **ResNet (2015)** broke that wall.

The fix is the **residual connection** (skip connection). Instead of asking a block of layers to compute a target mapping $H(x)$ directly, ResNet has it compute only the *residual* $F(x) = H(x) - x$ and then adds the input back:

$$ y = F(x) + x $$

If the ideal thing a block can do is "leave the input alone," it now only has to learn $F(x) = 0$ — driving weights to zero is easy — instead of learning to reproduce the identity through nonlinear layers, which is hard. The added bonus is that the $+x$ term gives gradients a direct, unobstructed path backward, largely curing the vanishing-gradient problem (see Chapter 15) and making 50-, 101-, even 152-layer networks trainable. Residual connections are now near-universal, well beyond vision.

- **EfficientNet (2019).** Observed that people scaled networks ad hoc — sometimes deeper, sometimes wider, sometimes higher-resolution. It proposed **compound scaling**: grow depth, width, and input resolution *together* in a fixed balanced ratio. The result is a family (B0–B7) hitting strong accuracy at a fraction of the compute of earlier models.

| Architecture | Year | Depth | Headline idea |
|---|---|---|---|
| LeNet-5 | 1998 | ~7 | the conv→pool→dense template |
| AlexNet | 2012 | 8 | ReLU + dropout + GPUs at scale |
| VGG | 2014 | 16–19 | stack only small 3×3 kernels |
| Inception | 2014 | 22 | parallel multi-scale, 1×1 bottlenecks |
| ResNet | 2015 | 50–152 | residual connections enable real depth |
| EfficientNet | 2019 | scaled | balanced compound scaling |

## 16.6 — Transfer learning with pretrained backbones

You rarely train a CNN from scratch. A network trained on ImageNet's 1.2 million images has already learned, in its early and middle layers, a general visual vocabulary — edges, textures, shapes — that transfers to almost any image task. **Transfer learning** reuses that.

The pretrained stack of conv layers is the **backbone** (or feature extractor). You take it, lop off its final classification head (which was specific to ImageNet's 1,000 classes), and attach a small new head for your task. Two common modes:

- **Feature extraction (freeze).** Freeze the backbone's weights, train only the new head. Fast, cheap, and the right default when your dataset is small — there isn't enough data to safely retrain millions of weights without overfitting.
- **Fine-tuning.** Unfreeze some or all of the backbone and continue training at a *low* learning rate so the pretrained features adjust gently to your domain. Better accuracy when you have more data, at more cost and more overfitting risk.

The intuition for *what* to freeze: early layers learn generic features that transfer everywhere, while later layers learn dataset-specific concepts. So if your data is close to natural photos, freeze less; if it is exotic (medical scans, satellite imagery), expect to fine-tune deeper. The general principle of adapting a large pretrained model to a downstream task recurs across the field — see the language version in Chapter 31.

::: {.callout-warning}
Two classic transfer-learning mistakes: using a high learning rate when fine-tuning, which wrecks the carefully-learned pretrained weights in the first few steps; and forgetting to apply the *same* input preprocessing (resize, normalization mean/std) the backbone was trained with — mismatched normalization silently tanks accuracy.
:::

## 16.7 — Regularization and data augmentation for CNNs

Big CNNs have millions of parameters and will happily memorize a training set if you let them. The two cheapest defences are so standard they are practically part of the architecture.

The first is **data augmentation**: manufacturing new training images by applying label-preserving transforms to the ones you have. Flip the image horizontally, crop a random sub-region, nudge the brightness, rotate a few degrees — the cat is still a cat, but the network now sees it from slightly different angles and never twice the same way. This directly reinforces the invariances we *want* (a cat is a cat regardless of position, scale, or lighting) and is often the single highest-leverage thing you can do when data is limited. Stronger schemes like *Cutout* (mask out a random patch) and *Mixup* (blend two images and their labels) push the idea further.

The second is **batch normalization**, which normalizes each channel's activations to roughly zero mean and unit variance within each mini-batch, then learns a scale and shift. Its main job is to stabilize and speed up training (Chapter 15 covers the mechanism), but it also has a mild regularizing side effect because the per-batch statistics inject a little noise. **Dropout** — randomly zeroing activations during training — is the other classic regularizer, though in convolutional layers it is largely superseded by batch norm and is mostly applied in the final dense head.

::: {.callout-tip}
If a CNN overfits, reach for more augmentation *before* you reach for a smaller model. Augmentation expands your effective dataset for free and rarely hurts; shrinking the network throws away capacity you may need.
:::

## 16.8 — CNNs beyond images, and the Vision Transformer contrast

Nothing about convolution requires a picture — it requires only data with a meaningful grid and local structure, in any number of dimensions.

- **1-D convolutions** slide a filter over a sequence: audio waveforms, sensor time series, and even text (a filter over a window of word embeddings detects local n-gram patterns). See Chapters 29 and 36.
- **3-D convolutions** add time or depth as a third axis: video (height × width × frames) and volumetric medical scans (CT, MRI).
- The same machinery underpins segmentation and detection architectures, where conv backbones feed task-specific heads.

For roughly a decade, CNNs *were* computer vision. Then the **Vision Transformer (ViT)** arrived, applying the transformer architecture (Chapter 18) to images by cutting them into patches and treating each patch as a token. The contrast is instructive:

- A CNN has strong **inductive biases** — locality and translation equivariance are hard-wired — so it learns efficiently from modest data. A ViT has almost no built-in spatial prior; it must *learn* that nearby patches relate, which means it needs very large datasets (or heavy augmentation and pretraining) to match CNN accuracy.
- A CNN's receptive field grows gradually with depth; a ViT's self-attention lets any patch attend to any other patch immediately, giving global context from layer one — an advantage for long-range relationships, at higher compute cost.

In practice the lines have blurred: modern systems borrow from both, and hybrid and convolution-flavoured transformers are common. CNNs remain the pragmatic default when data or compute is limited.

## 16.9 — Key takeaways

- Convolution wins on images through three priors: **local connectivity**, **parameter sharing**, and **translation equivariance** — together they cut parameters drastically and let one filter detect a pattern anywhere.
- **Equivariance** (output moves with the input) is what convolution gives directly; **invariance** (output unchanged) comes from pooling and global aggregation on top.
- A **kernel** sweeps the input to produce a **feature map**; **stride** and **padding** control output size via $W_{\text{out}} = \lfloor (W - K + 2P)/S \rfloor + 1$.
- Kernels span the full input **depth**; a layer holds many kernels, so spatial size shrinks while **channel** count grows — trading "where" for "what." **1×1 convs** mix channels cheaply.
- **Pooling** downsamples and adds small translation invariance; **global average pooling** is the standard, parameter-free bridge to the classifier; stacking layers grows the **receptive field** until deep neurons see whole objects.
- The arc LeNet → AlexNet → VGG → Inception → ResNet → EfficientNet is a story of going deeper; **residual connections** ($y = F(x) + x$) were the breakthrough that made real depth trainable.
- **Transfer learning** from a pretrained ImageNet **backbone** is the normal way to work — freeze for small data, fine-tune at a low learning rate for more.
- **Data augmentation** and **batch normalization** are the default regularizers; reach for more augmentation before a smaller model.
- Convolution generalizes to 1-D (audio, text, time series) and 3-D (video, volumes); **Vision Transformers** trade CNN inductive biases for data-hungry global attention.

## 16.10 — See also

- **Chapter 14 — Neural Network Fundamentals**: layers, activations, and backpropagation that CNNs build on.
- **Chapter 15 — Training Deep Neural Networks**: ReLU, dropout, batch norm, and the vanishing-gradient problem residual connections address.
- **Chapter 18 — Attention & Transformers**: the architecture behind Vision Transformers.
- **Chapter 27 — Computer Vision**: detection, segmentation, and full vision systems that use CNN backbones.
- **Chapter 29 — Speech & Audio Processing** and **Chapter 36 — Time Series Analysis & Forecasting**: 1-D convolution in practice.
- **Chapter 31 — Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment**: the transfer-learning idea applied to large language models.
- **Chapter 44 — Explainable AI & Interpretability**: visualizing what CNN filters and feature maps respond to.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 15 · ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks](ch15.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [17 · 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models →](ch17.html)
